The serpent's opening question—"Did God really say?"—cuts to the heart of every temptation that will follow. He does not demand that Eve disbelieve; he simply introduces doubt into her certainty. God's word becomes subject to examination, negotiation, reinterpretation. What follows is not crude deception but something far more dangerous: the serpent's promise that disobedience leads to enlightenment, that God is withholding something good, that transgression is actually liberation. Eve sees the fruit as genuinely desirable—not obviously evil but attractive, reasonable, wise. This is the weight of the moment: sin does not announce itself as darkness. It comes wearing the face of desire, dressed in the logic of self-improvement. And when Adam stands at the threshold with his wife and chooses to eat, he joins her not because he has been deceived but because he chooses her word over God's. The very one given to him as a helper becomes the instrument of his fall.

What happens next shows us the immediate fruit of disobedience. Their eyes open, yes—but not to wisdom. They discover shame. They rush to cover their nakedness with fig leaves, a feeble human solution to a problem that has become existential. Then comes the sound of God walking in the garden. Notice the absence of explanation or excuse in Adam's response: "I was afraid because I was naked." He does not say "I sinned" or "I disobeyed." His concern is exposure, judgment, the loss of standing before His Maker. God's question—"Who told you that you were naked?"—is not a request for information. It is an invitation to confession that Adam refuses. Instead, he blames the woman, and the woman blames the serpent. No one takes responsibility. No one falls on their knees asking for mercy.

But God does not leave them in their blame-shifting. His judgment is measured and terrible: the serpent is cursed, moving on its belly and eating dust; the woman will suffer pain and experience the fracturing of her relationship with her husband; the man will labor until death, eating bread by the sweat of his brow—a life of toil in a world that has turned hostile. Yet embedded within this judgment is a promise. God speaks of enmity between the woman's seed and the serpent's seed, and of one who will crush the serpent's head though his heel be struck. This is the first announcement of redemption: God will not leave creation in its ruin. And then, in a moment of profound grace, God Himself makes coverings of skin for Adam and Eve. Someone dies so that they might be clothed. Someone's blood covers their shame. It is a foreshadowing of the Lamb who would come, whose sacrifice would accomplish what fig leaves never could. The judgment is real. The consequence is permanent. But the last word belongs not to condemnation but to the promise of One who will undo what the serpent set in motion.
